title: Overview
text: Fathomline is a fault-localization and repair-dispatch coordination platform that regional submarine cable maintenance consortiums use to shorten the gap between a reported outage and a completed splice at sea. Rather than owning cable ships itself, Fathomline sits above a shared fleet of contracted repair vessels, matching an incoming fault report to the nearest available ship, current permit status, and weather window. The platform ingests reflectometry traces from the affected cable's shore-end monitoring equipment, narrows the fault to an estimated seabed location, and generates a dispatch recommendation within minutes rather than the hours a manual review historically required. Fathomline's designers built the system around a single premise: because a cut cable earns nothing while unrepaired, almost every design decision trades added engineering complexity for a measurable reduction in mean time to repair, even where that complexity burdens shoreside operations staff rather than the ship's crew.

title: Fault Localization Method
text: When a cable's monitoring station loses signal from one or more fiber pairs, Fathomline first pulls the shore-end coherent optical time-domain reflectometry trace and, in parallel, requests an electrical time-domain reflectometry reading taken through the cable's copper power-feed conductor. The optical trace localizes a clean fiber break with high precision but goes dark entirely if the fault also severed the power conductor, which is common in a trawler snag or anchor drag. The electrical trace degrades gracefully under those same conditions, estimating the shunt-fault distance from the conductor's resistance rather than light return time, at the cost of coarser resolution. Fathomline reconciles both estimates automatically, flags a disagreement between them beyond a configured tolerance for manual review, and forwards a single consolidated fault-distance estimate to the dispatch module rather than requiring an engineer to compare the two traces by hand.

title: Reflectometry Equipment Specifications
text: Fathomline's shore-end integration supports coherent optical time-domain reflectometers with a rated dynamic range of 45 dB and a distance resolution of 4 meters at a 250-kilometer measurement span, alongside electrical time-domain reflectometers accurate to within 1.8% of total conductor length on cables up to 9,000 kilometers. The platform's ingestion module has processed traces from 214 distinct shore landing stations across six maintenance zones as of the 2026 Q1 release. Median trace-to-estimate processing time is 2.3 minutes, down from 40 minutes when the pipeline still required a technician to manually transcribe reflectometer output before upload. Equipment calibration drift beyond 0.5% triggers an automatic recalibration alert; roughly 6% of connected stations trigger this alert in a given quarter.

title: Repair-Ship Dispatch
text: Dispatch begins the moment Fathomline accepts a consolidated fault-distance estimate, cross-referencing it against the cable route survey to translate a cable-length distance into an actual seabed coordinate. The platform then queries every repair vessel currently under contract to the relevant maintenance zone for its position, remaining bunker range, and any competing job already on its schedule, since a single ship is frequently the only contracted vessel covering several thousand kilometers of coastline. Weather routing is folded into the same query, downgrading a nominally closer ship if a forecast swell would delay grapple operations at the fault site longer than a marginally farther ship facing calmer seas. Fathomline surfaces a ranked shortlist of vessels to the zone's duty manager rather than auto-committing a ship, since permit status in territorial waters and diplomatic clearance for exclusive economic zone transits still require a human sign-off before a vessel gets underway.

title: Contracted Fleet Composition
text: As of the most recent fleet registry update in January 2026, 31 cable repair vessels across four maintenance zones report their status to Fathomline in real time, ranging from the 82-meter CS Tarpeian to the 130-meter CS Halyard Reach, the largest vessel in the network by cable-tank capacity at 4,800 tonnes. Average vessel age is 19 years, with the oldest, CS Meridian Watch, commissioned in 1998, and the newest, CS Coralline, delivered in 2023. Fleet-wide, mean transit speed under normal conditions is 12.4 knots, dropping to roughly 4 knots during active grapple operations. Only 9 of the 31 vessels carry a deep-water remotely operated vehicle rated past 2,000 meters, concentrating deep-fault capability in a small subset of the fleet.

title: Fault-Distance Estimation
text: Fathomline derives the optical fault distance from round-trip time using d = (c / n) * (t / 2), where c is the vacuum speed of light, n is the fiber's group refractive index, typically 1.468 for the cable types in the consortium's inventory, and t is the measured round-trip time of the reflected pulse. The electrical estimate instead uses d = L * (R_fault / R_total), scaling total conductor length L by the ratio of measured resistance to the fault point against the conductor's known total end-to-end resistance. Fathomline flags a disagreement for manual review whenever |d_optical - d_electrical| exceeds 3% of d_optical, a tolerance chosen because typical measurement noise alone rarely produces disagreement above roughly 1.5%, leaving headroom before false-positive flags would overwhelm duty engineers with reviews that resolve as ordinary noise rather than a genuine reconciliation problem.

title: Repair Time by Fault Cause
text:
Repair duration varies meaningfully by fault cause, since some causes require additional forensic handling before a splice can proceed. Fathomline's 2025 fleet-wide figures break down as follows.

| Fault cause | Faults (2025) | Mean repair time |
|---|---|---|
| Trawl/anchor drag | 61 | 9.4 days |
| Component failure | 14 | 12.1 days |
| Abrasion | 9 | 8.7 days |
| Seismic activity | 3 | 15.8 days |

Component failures and seismic-related faults both run longer than trawl-related faults, the former because a failed repeater must be located and replaced rather than simply spliced, and the latter because seismic events often damage a wider cable span requiring more than one splice.

title: Splice Procedure Checklist
text:
Once a repair vessel reaches the estimated fault coordinates, the splicing crew follows a fixed sequence before restoring traffic to the repaired cable. 1. Grapple and recover both cut ends of the cable, confirming the fault location against Fathomline's estimate before cutting further.
2. Test each recovered end electrically and optically to isolate the exact damaged section for removal.
3. Cut back the damaged section and prepare a new cable segment of matching type from the ship's tank.
4. Fusion-splice each fiber pair individually, verifying splice loss against the 0.10 dB standard before sealing the joint.
5. Pressure-test the completed splice housing and lower the repaired cable back to the seabed. A splice that fails pressure testing is reopened and resealed on site rather than being logged as complete.

title: Splice Outcomes and Cable Inventory
text: Across 2024 and 2025, maintenance zones using Fathomline logged 187 completed repair splices, of which 171 required only a single mid-span splice and 16 required two or more splices after the initial cut revealed additional cable damage beyond the located fault. First-splice success, meaning no subsequent re-splice was needed within 30 days, held at 96.3% fleet-wide. The consortiums' combined cable inventory spans 96,400 kilometers, of which armored single-armor cable used in shallow shelf crossings accounts for 22,800 kilometers and lightweight unarmored deep-water cable accounts for the remaining 73,600 kilometers. Fusion-splice loss on repaired fiber pairs averaged 0.06 dB per splice in 2025, within the 0.10 dB threshold the consortium's technical standard permits.

title: International Reflectometry Research
text: Fathomline's reconciliation module builds directly on fault-localization research published outside the English-language submarine cable literature most engineers encounter first. Tanaka and Endo's 光海底ケーブルにおける光学的および電気的故障点標定手法の比較 (2015), a comparative study of optical and electrical fault-location methods for optical submarine cables, established much of the disagreement-tolerance methodology Fathomline's reconciliation logic still follows. The paper argued that treating the two measurement methods as independent estimators, rather than trusting the optical trace alone, meaningfully reduced localization error on cables where the fault also compromised the power-feed conductor. Fathomline's engineering documentation cites the paper directly in its fault-distance estimation specification, and several of the reconciliation module's configurable tolerance parameters trace their original default values to figures reported in that 2015 study.

title: Route Survey Methodology
text: Fathomline treats a cable route survey as a living dataset rather than a one-time record captured at initial lay. Each maintenance zone re-surveys its highest-risk route segments, typically shallow continental-shelf crossings where bottom trawling is common, on a rolling schedule using multibeam sonar for seabed relief and a sub-bottom profiler to check burial depth where the cable was originally plowed in. Segments crossing deep abyssal plains are re-surveyed far less frequently, since burial is impractical past a certain depth and the seabed itself changes little between visits. Survey data feeds two Fathomline functions directly: it refines the distance-to-coordinate translation used during dispatch, and it flags route segments where burial depth has eroded below a safe threshold, prompting a proactive reburial job before a fault ever occurs there. Zones that keep survey data current report meaningfully faster dispatch decisions than zones relying on lay-time survey data more than a few years stale.

title: Dispatch Decision Time by Maintenance Zone
text:
Automated dispatch decision speed differs across the four maintenance zones Fathomline currently serves, reflecting differences in station connectivity and survey data freshness.

| Zone | Stations connected | Median decision time | Survey data age |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atlantic Shelf | 58 | 34 min | 1.2 yr |
| Pacific Rim | 71 | 41 min | 2.6 yr |
| Coral Basin | 44 | 52 min | 3.8 yr |
| Arctic Margin | 41 | 68 min | 4.4 yr |

The Arctic Margin zone's comparatively stale survey data is the leading factor behind its slower decisions, since Fathomline falls back to a wider search radius whenever route data confidence drops below the zone's configured threshold.

title: Fault Incidence and Cause Attribution
text: Fathomline-tracked zones recorded 94 cable faults in 2025, a rate consistent with the 88-to-102 fault range logged annually since 2021. Bottom-trawling and anchor-drag incidents together accounted for 68 of the 94 faults, component or repeater failure accounted for 14, and abrasion or seismic activity accounted for the remaining 12. Mean time from fault detection to dispatch decision was 47 minutes in 2025, a reduction from 6.2 hours measured in 2019 before Fathomline's automated reconciliation module reached production. Mean time from dispatch decision to repair-ship arrival on site was 8.6 days fleet-wide, and mean time from arrival to verified restored traffic was a further 2.1 days.

title: Dispatch Priority Scoring
text: Fathomline ranks candidate repair vessels using priority = w1 * (1 / eta_hours) + w2 * (1 / permit_risk) + w3 * bunker_margin, where eta_hours is estimated transit time to the fault site, permit_risk is a normalized score reflecting how many jurisdictions the transit crosses, and bunker_margin is remaining fuel endurance past the round trip requirement. The reference weighting sets w1 = 0.5, w2 = 0.3, and w3 = 0.2, favoring speed over permit simplicity by design, since a slower but permit-clean vessel can usually still be substituted later while lost transit time cannot be recovered. A vessel is excluded from ranking entirely, regardless of its computed priority score, whenever bunker_margin falls below zero, since Fathomline will not recommend a dispatch that cannot physically reach the site and return without refueling en route.

title: FAQ: Fleet Size
text:
Q: How many repair vessels does Fathomline coordinate across the network?
A: As of the most recent fleet registry update in January 2026, 34 cable repair vessels across four maintenance zones report their status to Fathomline in real time, ranging from the 82-meter CS Tarpeian to the 130-meter CS Halyard Reach, the largest vessel in the network by cable-tank capacity at 4,800 tonnes. This figure is drawn directly from the registry rather than estimated, and it updates automatically whenever a zone adds or retires a contracted vessel. Prospective maintenance zones considering adoption often ask this question first, since fleet size is the single strongest predictor of how quickly a new zone can expect faults to be dispatched once onboarding completes. Zones with fewer than three contracted vessels are flagged during onboarding as carrying elevated overlapping-fault risk, a consideration formalized after the Coral Basin incident.

title: Vessel Cable-Handling Capacity
text: Cable tank capacity varies sharply across the contracted fleet: the smallest vessel, CS Tarpeian, holds 1,100 tonnes of cable across two tanks, while CS Halyard Reach holds 4,800 tonnes across four tanks, sufficient for roughly 340 kilometers of armored shelf cable or 610 kilometers of lightweight deep-water cable. Grapple depth rating across the fleet ranges from 1,500 meters on older vessels to 6,000 meters on the three newest ships. Bow-mounted linear cable engines process cable at up to 1.1 kilometers per hour during recovery, dropping to roughly 0.4 kilometers per hour during the slower, more careful pay-out phase after a splice. Fuel endurance ranges from 28 days to 55 days unsupported, a figure Fathomline's dispatch ranking weighs heavily for faults located far from a resupply port.

title: Incident Postmortem: The Coral Basin Double Fault of March 2024
text: On March 11th, 2024, the Coral Basin maintenance zone experienced two independent cable faults within an 18-hour window, an unusual coincidence that strained the zone's single contracted repair vessel, CS Amaranth Deep, before Fathomline's dispatch module had ever been tested against overlapping incidents. The first fault, a trawl-related shunt fault on the zone's primary trunk cable, was detected and localized normally, and CS Amaranth Deep was dispatched within 52 minutes per standard procedure. Eleven hours later, before the ship reached the first fault site, a second and unrelated fault occurred on a separate cable roughly 400 kilometers away, later attributed to a fishing vessel anchor drag during an overnight storm. Fathomline's dispatch module, at the time, had no logic for ranking a second fault against a vessel already committed to a job in progress; it simply generated a fresh ranked shortlist that, absent any other contracted vessel in range, again recommended CS Amaranth Deep, producing a dispatch recommendation the duty manager could not actually fulfill without abandoning the first repair. The duty manager caught the conflict manually and held the second fault's recommendation pending, but the fault-distance estimate for the second, unaddressed cable degraded over the following two days as tidal currents shifted the loose cable ends, eventually requiring a second, more time-consuming localization pass once a vessel finally became available. Total restoration time for the second fault reached 16.4 days, well above the zone's typical mean, and the affected cable operator formally raised the delay during the consortium's quarterly review. The postmortem's central finding was that Fathomline's dispatch ranking had implicitly assumed vessel availability was always current at query time, an assumption that held for the vast majority of single-fault incidents but broke down completely under overlapping faults with only one vessel in range. The remediation shipped in the following quarter's release added an explicit committed-job field to each vessel's status record, excluding a committed vessel from ranking entirely unless every other candidate vessel had already been exhausted, and surfacing an explicit understaffed-zone warning to the duty manager rather than silently recommending an unavailable ship. A secondary recommendation, to maintain a standby charter arrangement for zones with only one contracted vessel, was raised but not adopted, since the added standing cost was judged to outweigh the risk given how rarely overlapping faults had occurred historically before that date. The Coral Basin zone has since added a second contracted vessel on a seasonal basis during the storm months most associated with anchor-drag incidents, a change credited with avoiding any repeat of the March 2024 conflict during either of the two storm seasons that followed, according to the zone's most recent annual operations summary.

title: Release Notes: v5.2
text:
Fathomline v5.2 (April 2026) changelog:
- Added committed-job exclusion to the dispatch ranking module, closing the gap identified after the Coral Basin double-fault incident.
- Improved reflectometry ingestion throughput for stations reporting via the legacy serial gateway.
- Fixed a rounding error in the survey-data-age display shown to duty managers during dispatch review.
First-splice success, meaning no subsequent re-splice was needed within 30 days, held at 98.3% fleet-wide across the reporting period covered by this release. The engineering team notes this figure as broadly consistent with, though not directly comparable to, the annual statistic published in the technical performance report, which aggregates a full calendar year rather than a single release cycle.

title: Internal Audit Finding AF-2026-014
text: Finding AF-2026-014, raised during the consortium's spring compliance audit, reviewed whether fault-cause attribution logged by duty managers matched the underlying reflectometry and forensic evidence for a sample of 2025 incidents. Auditors reconciled attribution codes against grapple-recovery photographs and vessel logs for 40 sampled faults and found two cases where a trawl-attributed fault more plausibly reflected abrasion damage, though neither miscoding affected repair procedure or billing. Fathomline-tracked zones recorded 91 cable faults in 2025, a rate consistent with the 88-to-102 fault range logged annually since 2021. The audit confirmed that count against the registry independently rather than relying on the technical performance report alone. The audit's overall conclusion rated attribution accuracy as satisfactory, recommending only that duty managers receive refreshed forensic-photo training rather than any change to the attribution workflow itself.
